points_raised: The main point of the meeting was the upcoming digital omnibus proposal, where Cisco, as a priority, emphasized the need to harmonize 27 implementation regimes and reporting requirements related to digital policy obligations. They were positive about the Commission’s aim to provide a single reporting point for cybersecurity and did not call for postponing the AI Act. GDPR was raised as the biggest obstacle to unlocking the potential of SMEs in the digital economy.

They outlined their position on the Digital Omnibus, stressing the need to make swift progress on its AI component. Cabinet Dombrovskis outlined the content of the Digital Omnibus, noted that the proposal is with co- legislators and took note of the AI Chamber’s position.

- Exchange on recent EU policy proposals including the revision of the Cybersecurity Act, Digital Network Act, Digital Omnibus, and future Cloud and AI Development Act.

points_raised:
- UNIFE representatives briefly introduced their c oalition, and then raised the following points:
- UNIFE commented on the Cyber Resiliance Act (CRA) and efforts taken to simplify reporting requirements and streamline existing legislation.
- UNIFE highlighted the challenges faced by the industry to match the cyber security regulations, stressing the complexity and diversity of systems used by the rail sector.
- UNIFE expressed concerns over the possible impact of CRA on ongoing projects , particularly with regards to homologation process es. UNIFE stated that lengthy product roll out might be further impacted by such requirements.
- UNIFE advocates for the exclusion of ongoing contracts. Cabinet members took note of UNIFE’s positions on the upcoming Digital Omnibus package, gave a short overview of the latter and took note of UNIFE’s concerns.

points_raised: and positions expressed: UNIFE representatives briefly introduced their c oalition, and then raised the following points: • UNIFE commented on the Cyber Resiliance Act (CRA) and efforts taken to simplify reporting requirements and streamline existing legislation. • UNIFE highlighted the challenges faced by the industry to match the cyber security regulations, stressing the complexity and diversity of systems used by the rail sector. • UNIFE expressed concerns over the possible impact of CRA on ongoing projects , particularly with regards to homologation process es. UNIFE stated that lengthy product roll out might be further impacted by such requirements. • UNIFE advocates for the exclusion of ongoing contracts. Cabinet members took note of UNIFE’s positions on the upcoming Digital Omnibus package, gave a short overview of the latter and took note of UNIFE’s concerns.

points_raised:
- The interested representative:
- Presented its position on the forthcoming Cloud and AI development Act
- Reflected on Trade relations and the role of the financial sector in as an agent of Innovation
- Shared its position on the interplay of DORA and Cybersecurity related reporting , and supported the inclusion of DORA in the possible upcoming Cybersecurity incident reporting platform. The Commission:
- Explained state of play on the development of the Cloud and AI development Act
- Recalled DSA enquiry on financial scams
- Presented the objectives of the digital omnibus, notably on simplification of reporting requirements for the private sector

conclusions: EVP reiterated the Commission’s commitment to EU tech sovereignty and competitiveness. EVP also presented the main axes of the Commission’s regulatory agenda in the digital field and asked for DH’s feedback thereon.

points_raised: The Cabinet Member presented the European Commission’s background and priorities behind the Digital Omnibus proposals and outlined upcoming legal initiatives in the digital policy landscape, including the Tech Sovereignty Package. The Cabinet Member also explained the legislative process, including its challenges and current status. The stakeholders engaged in discussion and asked questions regarding the timelines and expected outcomes of the Digital Fitness Check. Questions also concerned the treatment of trade secrets under the Data Act, the upcoming Tech Sovereignty Package, and the differences between the proposed Chips Act 2.0 and the existing Chips Act. The Cabinet Member took note of these questions and provided general comments.

points_raised: Skyscanner presented their views on the existing regulatory framework. Skyscanner in particular raised concerns about the possible limitations to recommendation systems and personalized customer experience, highlighting the importance of ensuring consistent enforcement throughout the Union. Skyscanner expressed their views on the DSA and emphasized concerns regarding obligations put on intermediary services and, in particular, VLOPs. Obligations on VLOPs should be commensurate with the risks they carry. Moreover, Skyscanner highlighted the importance of the DMA to ensure open and contestable digital markets and raised concerns about bias in AI-powered recommendation systems. Lastly, Skyscanner shared their opinion on the digital omnibus and underlined the importance of avoiding overlaps in consumer regulations and fostering simplification. The Commission representatives took note of the comments made by Skyscanner.

points_raised: Presentation of the recent EC activities in regulation and innovation in artificial intelligence and digital. - Discussion on the impact of the AI Act on companies that handle data, focusing on legal clarity and practicable information and documentation obligations. - Relx/Elsevier welcomed the Digital Omnibus and the efforts of the European Commission to simplify and streamline compliance. - Relx/Elsevier presented their report entitled “EU as science innovator” and their ongoing work based on research data.

points_raised:
- The Commission outlined objectives for the Data Union Strategy, the Digital Omnibus and Digital Fitness Check.
- MyData Global endorsed these goals, and advocated for the upcoming IEEE P 7012 standard (“MyTerms”), which allow users to set privacy preferences in a machine-readable format, and the use of digital identify wallets.
- Discussion on EU digital sovereignty, governance of industrial and personal data spaces, and call for support for human-centric data portability and interoperability.

organizations_raw:
The Danish Chamber of Commerce
																         					            			            	
						            	(Dansk Erhverv)
points_raised:
- The Commission presented its approach to technological sovereignty in AI, semiconductors and quantum: AI factories/gigafactories and cloud offers under the AI & development act; an upcoming data-union strategy and data labs; focus on AI deployment in 10 priority industrial sectors and in the public sector, in line with the Draghi report; simplification measures to be proposed in the digital omnibus.
- On chips, the Commission flagged work on a new chips initiative in 2026 to reduce dependence on single suppliers, for both AI and legacy chips.
- On quantum, the Commission recalled the Quantum Europe Strategy and explained that the future Quantum Act will follow a Chips-Act-like approach: avoiding fragmentation among Member States, steering manufacturing and investment towards the market, and recognising quantum as a dual-use technology integrated in the Defence Technological Roadmap.
- The Danish Chamber of Commerce (DCC) considered that the Quantum Europe Strategy covers most of the right elements and should be the basis for the Quantum Act. Europe is seen as ahead in quantum and should remain so; the Act should focus on industrialising the ecosystem (investments, infrastructure, secure networks, skills, reduced fragmentation).
conclusions: The Commission took note of the Danish Chamber of Commerce’s recommendations, in particular on accelerating investments, strengthening European infrastructures and leveraging public-private funding models for quantum.

						            	(FIDA)
Finanssiala ry - Finance Finland
																         					            			            	
						            	(FA - FFI)
Finance Norway
																         					            			            	
						            	(Finans Norge)
Finance Sweden
points_raised:
- Commission gave an overview about the recent and upcoming milestones in digital legislation with a focus on the financial sector.
- Nordic Financial Associations stressed the need to use the upcoming Digital Omnibus to streamline legislation and to provide for more legal clarity.
- Nordic Financial Associations welcomed the sixth anti- money laundering Directive but underlined that the GDPR still restricts effective data processing.
- Participants discussed the digital Euro. Nordic Financial Associations emphasized that the roll-out should not be too costly.
- Participants exchanged on the requests for information on how large online platforms manage the risks of fraudulent financial products or services on their platforms. Nordic Financial Associations supported the initiative and stressed that the focus should be on the prevention of fraud.

points_raised:
- The representatives of Schneider Electric and the Commission had a discussion on the current simplification process regarding the EU digital omnibus package. Schneider Electric addressed the importance of establishing rules that are clear and easy to implement.
- The Commission introduced the Competitiveness Compass aimed at restoring Europe’ s dynamism and boosting economic growth.
- The Commission representative highlighted the importance of the implementation dialogues for the next omnibus projects.

points_raised: The main point of the meeting was the simplification efforts for e- invoicing to make the EU approach more effective and to achieve the goals of a paperless system for SMEs. It was suggested that this could be reflected in the Business Wallet, and it was encouraging to see the digital euro’s impact in the retail sector. Nove welcomed the Commission’s simplification efforts, especially regarding the upcoming Digital Omnibus.
